Transport, communication and trade establish links between producing centres and consuming centres. The system of mass production and exchange is complex. Each region... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: TRANSPOR TRANSPOR TRANSPOR TRANSPOR TRANSPORT T T T T, URBAN TRANSPORT SOLUTIONS, WATER TRANSPORT, PIPELINES. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Tertiary and Quaternary Activities is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that fundamentals of Human Geography 54 Unit-III Chapter-7 Transport and Communication Natural resources, economic activities and markets are rarely found in one place.

The text explains that transport, communication and trade establish links between producing centres and consuming centres.

The text explains that the system of mass production and exchange is complex.

The text explains that each region produces the items for which it is best suited.

The text explains that trade or the exchange of such commodities relies on transportation and communication.

The text explains that likewise, the high living standards and quality of life depend on efficient transportation, communications and trade.

The text explains that in earlier days, the means of transport and communication were the same.

The text explains that but today both have acquired distinct and specialised forms.

The text explains that transport provides the network of links and carriers through which trade takes place.

The text explains that tRANSPOR TRANSPOR TRANSPOR TRANSPOR TRANSPORT T T T T Transport is a service or facility for the carriage of persons and goods from one place to the other using humans, animals and different kinds of vehicles.

The text explains that such movements take place over land, water and air.

The text explains that roads and railways form part of land transport; while shipping and waterways and airways are the other two modes.

The text explains that pipelines carry materials like petroleum, natural gas, and ores in liquidified form.

The text explains that moreover, transportation is an organised service industry created to satisfy the basic needs of society.

The text explains that it includes transport arteries, vehicles to carry people and goods, and the organisation to maintain arteries, and to handle loading, unloading and delivery.

The text explains that every nation has developed various kinds of transportation for defence purposes.

The text explains that in a well-managed transport system, these various modes complement each other.

The text explains that it opened up continental interiors for commercial grain farming, mining and manufacturing in U.S.A.

The text explains that liquids like mineral oil, water, sludge and sewers are transported by pipelines.

The text explains that they are important in supplementing modern channels and carriers which penetrate the interiors in large countries.

The text explains that but unfortunately, the world’s road system is not well developed.

The text explains that the highest road density and the highest number of vehicles are registered in this continent compared to Western Europe.

The text explains that 7.3 : Dharmavaram Tuni National Highway, India In North America, highway density is high, about 0.65 km per sq km.

The text explains that belgium has the highest density of 1 km of railway for every 6.5 sq kms area.

The text explains that fundamentals of Human Geography 58 their importance to quicker and more flexible transport systems of airways and roadways.

The text explains that only Chile, among the remaining countries has a considerable route length linking coastal centres with the mining sites in the interior.

The text explains that peru, Bolivia, Ecuador, Colombia and Venezuela have short single-track rail-lines from ports to the interior with no inter-connecting links.

The text explains that elsewhere, as in Algeria, Senegal, Nigeria, Kenya and Ethiopia, railway lines connect port cities to interior centres but do not form a good network with other countries.

The text explains that they were constructed for economic and political reasons to facilitate long runs in different directions.

The text explains that 7.8: The view of Seine River from the Eiffel Tower (One can see how the river has Eiffel Tower (One can see how the river has Eiffel Tower (One can see how the river has Eiffel Tower (One can see how the river has Eiffel Tower (One can see how the river has become an important Inland waterway) become an important Inland waterway) become an important Inland waterway) become an important Inland waterway) become an important Inland waterway) Sea Routes Sea Routes Sea Routes Sea Routes Sea Routes The oceans offer a smooth highway traversable in all directions with no maintenance costs.

The text explains that the volume of trade and traffic between both East and West Africa is on the increase due to the development of the rich natural resources such as gold, diamond, copper, tin, groundnut, oil palm, coffee and fruits.

The text explains that there is also little traffic on the route between Rio de Janeiro and Cape Town because both South America and Africa have similar products and resources.

The text explains that the direct route on the Great Circle links Vancouver and Yokohama and reduces the travelling distance (2,480 km) by half.

The text explains that it gives Europe a new gateway to the Indian Ocean and reduces direct sea-route distance between Liverpool and Colombo compared to the Cape of Good Hope route.

The text explains that it has been constructed across the Panama Isthmus between Panama City and Colon by the U.S. government which purchased 8 km of area on either side and named it the Canal Zone.

The text explains that it has a six- lock system and ships cross the different levels (26 m up and down) through these locks before entering the Gulf of Panama.

The text explains that 7.12: Inland waterways are a major source of transport wherever the river is wide, deep and free of silt The significance of rivers as inland waterways for domestic and international Fig.

The text explains that in U.S.A. there is a dense network of oil pipelines from the producing areas to the The Mississippi Waterways The Mississippi-Ohio waterway connects the interior part of U.S.A. with the Gulf of Mexico in the south.

The text explains that 7.15: An Aeroplane at Salsburg Airport The manufacturing of aircrafts and their operations require elaborate infrastructure like hangars, landing, fuelling, and maintenance facilities for the aircrafts.

The text explains that fundamentals of Human Geography Fundamentals of Human Geography Fundamentals of Human Geography Fundamentals of Human Geography Fundamentals of Human Geography 68 68 68 68 68 over the world soon upgraded their copper cable systems to include optic fiber cables.

The text explains that artificial satellites, now, are successfully deployed in the earth’s orbit to connect even the remote corners of the globe with limited on- site verification.

The text explains that on 19 June 1981, APPLE (Arian Passenger Payload Experiment) was launched through Arian rocket.

The text explains that the speed at which this electronic network has spread is unprecedented in human history.

The text explains that it is these modern communication systems, more than transportation, that has made the concept of global village a reality.

The text explains that choose the right answer from the four alternatives given below.

The text explains that john’s City (d) Chengdu and Lhasa (ii) Which country has the highest density of railway network?

The text explains that (i) Elucidate the statement– “In a well managed transport system, various modes complement each other”.

The text explains that (i) The Trans–Continental Stuart Highway runs between (a) Darwin and Melbourne (b) Edmonton and Anchorage (c) Vancouver and St.

The text explains that (a) Brazil (c) Canada (b) U.S.A (d) Russia (iii) The Big Trunk Route runs through (a) The Mediterranean – Indian ocean (b) The North Atlantic Ocean (c) The South Atlantic Ocean (d) The North Pacific Ocean (iv) The Big Inch pipeline transports (a) Milk (c) Water (b) Liquid petroleum gas (LGP) (d) Petroleum (v) Which one pair of the following places is linked by Channel Tunnel?

The text explains that (a) London – Berlin (c) Berlin – Paris (b) Paris – London (d) Barcelona – Berlin 2.

The text explains that (i) What are the problems of road transport in mountainous, desert and flood prone regions?

The text explains that (iii) What are the advantages of water transport?
